coredata.dc:title: Coffee grounds carbon quantum dots modified ZnO nanorods for Near-UV photodetectors
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yitao
Luo, Zhiqiang
Li, Jinghui
Yu, Xuan
Yu, Xiaoming
Qiao, Qian
Zhang, Hai
Li, Zhenhua
Zhou, Yingtang
coredata.dc:description: Near-ultraviolet photodetectors (NUV PDs) are widely used in various applications such as autonomous driving, UV radiation monitoring, and medical diagnostics. ZnO is a low-cost, high-performance semiconductor material for UV light detection. However, solution-processed ZnO thin films exhibit poor conductivity, high density of defects and insufficient light absorption, which limits the development and application of ZnO-based NUV PDs. Herein, we have developed a novel, eco-friendly, and efficient method for extracting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from discarded coffee grounds, named as CCQDs, and successfully integrated them into ZnO nanorods (NRs) for the first time to improve NUV PDs. The CCQDs modified ZnO NRs show a boosting light scattering with an increased haze by 120.0 % in 300–420 nm, and enhanced conductivity enable a lowered dark current and a higher photo current. The resulting NUV PDs exhibits a significant improvement in the ratio of Iphoto/Idark by 3467.3 % and a relatively sizeable normalized detection (D*: 9.81 × 1010 Jones), high linear dynamic range (LDR: 65.32 dB), and low equivalent noise power (NEP: 2.88 × 10−12 W) compared to the intrinsic ZnO NRs device. The outstanding performance can be attributed to the increased light absorption, reduced defects of ZnO NRs after the modification of CCQDs, and the faster electron transfer on the ZnO NRs/CCQDs thin films. This work provides a simple method for developing low-cost, high-performance ZnO-based PDs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Coffee grounds
Near-UV
Photodetector
Thin film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) The process flowchart for preparing CCQDs. (b) Device preparation process diagram.
(a–d) SEM of the samples ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/CCQDs. (e–f) EDS spectra of ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/CCQDs thin films respectively. (g–h) EDS mapping of ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/CCQDs.

(a) XRD patterns of FTO, ZnO NRs thin films and ZnO NRs/CCQDs hybrid thin films. (b) Total transmittance. (c) Specular transmittance. (d) Haze value. (e) Absorption and (f) (αhν) 2 - hν function curve obtained from absorption.

(a) Logarithmic plot of I-V curves of the two devices under dark conditions. (b) Logarithmic plot of I-V curves of the two devices illuminated with a light of 380 nm. (c) Photo-response of devices with and without CCQDs under NUV (380 nm) illumination at an intensity of 10 mW/cm2. (d) Photocurrent of devices with and without CCQDs under change light-intensity illumination. (e) The ratio of Iphoto/Idark. (f) Rise-fall time. Performance testing of the devices: (g) D*, (h) LDR, (i) NEP.
Iphoto/Idark, R, D*, LDR, NEP of with CCQDs and without CCQDs under the light of 380 nm.

head.ce:title: Coffee grounds carbon quantum dots modified ZnO nanorods for Near-UV photodetectors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Marine Engineering Equipment, Zhejiang Ocean University, Zhoushan, Zhejiang, 316022, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Zhejiang Ocean University, Zhoushan, Zhejiang, 316022, China.
Corresponding author. Zhejiang Ocean University, Zhoushan, Zhejiang, 316022, China.
Corresponding author. Zhejiang Ocean University, Zhoushan, Zhejiang, 316022, China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Near-ultraviolet photodetectors (NUV PDs) are widely used in various applications such as autonomous driving, UV radiation monitoring, and medical diagnostics. ZnO is a low-cost, high-performance semiconductor material for UV light detection. However, solution-processed ZnO thin films exhibit poor conductivity, high density of defects and insufficient light absorption, which limits the development and application of ZnO-based NUV PDs. Herein, we have developed a novel, eco-friendly, and efficient method for extracting carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from discarded coffee grounds, named as CCQDs, and successfully integrated them into ZnO nanorods (NRs) for the first time to improve NUV PDs. The CCQDs modified ZnO NRs show a boosting light scattering with an increased haze by 120.0 % in 300–420 nm, and enhanced conductivity enable a lowered dark current and a higher photo current. The resulting NUV PDs exhibits a significant improvement in the ratio of Iphoto/Idark by 3467.3 % and a relatively sizeable normalized detection (D*: 9.81 × 1010 Jones), high linear dynamic range (LDR: 65.32 dB), and low equivalent noise power (NEP: 2.88 × 10−12 W) compared to the intrinsic ZnO NRs device. The outstanding performance can be attributed to the increased light absorption, reduced defects of ZnO NRs after the modification of CCQDs, and the faster electron transfer on the ZnO NRs/CCQDs thin films. This work provides a simple method for developing low-cost, high-performance ZnO-based PDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: UV PDs are devices used to detect and measure UV light. They are designed to convert UV light into electrical signals that can be measured or used for various applications [1–5]. UV PDs can be classified into Far-UV PDs (100–280 nm), Mid-UV PDs (280–320 nm), and NUV PDs (320–400 nm) based on wavelength [6]. Among them, NUV PDs are widely used in fields such as autonomous driving, UV radiation monitoring, and medical diagnostics due to their high sensitivity, fast response, and wide wavelength range [7–10]. ZnO, a comprehensive bandgap semiconductor material with a bandgap of approximately 3.37 eV [11–15]. It has high exciton binding energy, a broad range of bandgap tuning, and intense radiation resistance, making it an ideal material for NUV PDs [16–18]. ZnO NRs prepared through a simple, low-cost, and controllable hydrothermal method provide a direct pathway for charge carrier transport, thereby reducing the average transfer distance and suppressing recombination [19]. Additionally, the nanorod structure facilitates light capture and increases light absorption [20,21]. For example, Yu et al. applied ZnO NRs to thin films to change the surface morphology and thus increase the haze value and light scattering property [22]. Yu et al. controlled the light scattering properties of ZnO NRs through the gradual annealing rate of the seed layer [23]. Although ZnO NRs can effectively trap light, interface modification is still required to reduce defects, enhance carrier mobility, and improve light utilization [24]. Zhou et al. used ZnO QDs to modify ZnO NRAs, increasing light scattering [25]. Inspired by this, we experimentally found that using CCQDs as the interface modifier for ZnO NRs thin films can better control light scattering.

The earliest CQDs were accidently synthesized by the scientist Xiaoyou Xu in 2004. They were initially applied in fluorescent imaging and bio-labelling fields. With further research, it has been discovered that CQDs as a low-cost carbon-based nanomaterial, possess advantages such as wide bandgap, high sensitivity, and low noise, making them highly suitable for application in the field of UV photoelectric detectors. For instance, Nawrzhan Serkjan et al. introduced CQDs into PDs to significantly improve device responsivity [26]. Wang et al. significantly reduced the response time of devices by modifying ZnO thin films with CQDs [27]. Meanwhile, some researchers have synthesized CQDs using biomass materials [28–31]. For example, S. Thambiraj et al. synthesized highly fluorescent CQDs through green synthesis using sugarcane bagasse [32], and Wang et al. enhanced the photocatalytic activity of Bi3Ti4O12 nanosheets using CQDs prepared from bamboo [33]. Gwajeong Jeong et al. synthesized CQDs from discarded coffee grounds via ball milling and applied them in fluorescence chemical sensors [34]. However, the application of CQDs prepared from biomass materials in photoelectric detectors is rarely reported.

In this work, we prepared CCQDs from discarded coffee grounds (CGs) and, for the first time, used CCQDs to modify ZnO NRs thin films. Using CCQDs as surface modifiers has three advantages. Firstly, CCQDs are prepared from waste CGs, which makes the preparation process simple and cost-effective. Secondly, CCQDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility, chemical inertness, and ease of surface functionalization [35–40]. Lastly, the 0-dimensional CCQDs can effectively modify the defects of 1-dimensional ZnO NRs, resulting in a significant improvement in the performance of the prepared devices. The use of CCQDs as interface decorations significantly increases the light scattering of the thin films, leading to substantial improvements in the overall performance of the PDs. For instance, under the most favorable conditions, the D* value increased significantly by 571.9 % from 1.46 × 1010 to 9.81 × 1010 Jones, the LDR improved by 90.1 % to 65.32 dB, and the NEP decreased by 85.1 % to 2.88 × 10−12 W, compared to the intrinsic ZnO NRs devices. This powerfully demonstrates the potential of CQDs to become an essential choice for future optoelectronic detector materials.

First, the CGs (purchased from Luckin Coffee (China) Co., Ltd) were utterly dried in an oven (80 °C, 48 h). As shown in Fig. 1 . (a), 2.5 g of CGs sample was placed in a beaker and heated at 200 °C in an oven for 4 h. The heated samples were subsequently dissolved in deionized water and subjected to magnetic stirring (60 °C, 10 min). The water solution was separated from solids by centrifugation (6000 rpm, 30 min) to extract the supernatant. Finally, a yellow, high-purity CCQDs solution was obtained by continuous dialysis (MWCO: 500 Da) for 2 h.

The FTO substrate was treated with glass cleaner, alcohol, and deionized water sequentially for 15 min, followed by drying and plasma treatment for 5 min to obtain the substrate. ZnO prepared by sol-gel method was spin-coated on FTO (600 r/min 9 s and 2000 r/min 20 s), and then annealed at 245 °C/40 min and 385 °C/1 h. The preparation method of ZnO NRs refers to our previous work [41]. Then, the prepared CCQDs were spin-coated on ZnO NRs thin films (3000 r/min 30 s), repeated 5 times, and dried in air at 60 °C for 5 min. The active layer (15 mg poly(3-hexylthiophene) P3HT, (purchased from Advanced Election Technology Co., Ltd) was spin-coated on the substrate and annealed at 125 °C/15 min. Finally, molybdenum trioxide (MoO3 3 nm) and silver electrode (120 nm) were vacuum evaporated to complete the final device preparation. The entire device preparation process is shown in Fig. 1. (b).

The thin film's surface morphology was examined using a scanning electron microscope (SEM) model SUPRA55VP. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/CCQDs composite thin films were measured by the X-ray diffractometer (HaoYuan, DX2700). The light transmission and absorption spectra were measured in the range of 300–420 nm by a spectrophotometer (SHIMADZU, UV-2600). The current-voltage (I-V) characteristics and transient photoresponse characteristics of detectors were measured by the Keithley 2400 source meter under the illumination of 380 nm LED light (10 mW/cm2).

From Fig. 2 (a-d) , it can be seen that the ZnO NRs grown vertically on the FTO substrate via hydrothermal method are uniformly dense, and it indicates that the modification with CCQDs has not affected the morphological characteristics of the ZnO NRs. The EDS spectra in Fig. 2 (e-f) correspond to the ZnO NRs and ZnO NRs/CCQDs thin films, respectively. It is observed that the carbon content is as high as 21.5 %, which proves the successful modification of the ZnO NRs thin films by CCQDs. The elemental mapping test confirmed the successful fabrication of the ZnO NRs/CCQDs thin films, as shown in Fig. 2 (g-h). The color images display the mapping of Zn, O, and C elements, revealing their uniform distribution.

Fig. 3 . (a) shows the XRD patterns in the range 20°–70°. We can see that both samples have firm (002) diffraction peaks at approximately 34.4°, indicating that they preferentially grow in the (002) direction [42]. To verify the effect of CCQDs modification on the performance of ZnO NRs, we analyze the Total transmittance (Tt), Specular transmittance (St), and Absorption (Abs) of the thin films. From Fig. 3. (b, c, e), it can be observed that the CCQDs modification has a significant impact on the thin films, with a noticeable change occurring around a wavelength of 370 nm. In the range of 300–370 nm, the Tt and St values of the samples modified by CCQDs are slightly higher than those of the pristine ZnO NRs except for the Abs value. The slight increase in Tt and St values may be attributed to the light scattering and absorption effects of CCQDs, as well as the modification of the surface morphology and structural features of ZnO NRs by CCQDs, thereby influencing their optical properties [43–45]. A lower transmittance is observed in the range of 370–420 nm, primarily due to the enhanced light scattering and increased light absorption resulting from the CCQDs modification, leading to a decrease in transmittance. At the same time, there is a certain degree of enhancement in the absorption, indicating the role of CCQDs-modified ZnO NRs in trapping light. The light scattering characteristics can be described by haze value:
(1)
H a z e =
T
t o t a l
−
T
s p e c t r u m
T
t o t a l
* 100 %

Hazes of the two devices are shown in Fig. 3. (d). Overall, the haze value of CCQDs-modified thin films significantly increases in the 320–400 nm near-UV region. The haze value of the thin films increases by 120.0 % after CCQDs modification, directly indicating the intense light scattering properties of CCQDs [46,47]. We calculated the optical bandgap of the thin films from absorption curve by the Tauc formula [48]:
(2)
(
α h ν
)
1 / n
= A
(
h ν
−
E g
)
Where α is the absorbance index, h is Planck's constant,
ν
is the frequency, A is a constant, and Eg is the bandgap of the semiconductor [49]. From Fig. 3. (f), it can be seen that the optical bandgap of the sample with CCQDs modification decreased from 3.96 eV to 3.92 eV. This indicates that CCQDs can improve light absorption and enhance light utilization efficiency [50,51].

The photovoltaic (P-N) characteristics of the P3HT-coated thin films with spin-coated CCQDs were measured under bias voltages between −2 V and 2 V, showing both photo state and dark state I-V characteristics in Fig. 4 . (a,b). The rectification ratio is the ratio of current obtained at ±2 V. In the dark state I-V curve, it can be observed that the P-N junction without CCQDs exhibits relatively poor rectification performance and a significantly high surface leakage current [52]. However, after the introduction of CCQDs, the rectification ratio increases considerably from 11.96 to 6469.60, and the surface leakage current decreases dramatically from 152.36 to 0.13 mA. The photo state I-V curve also exhibits the same characteristics. This indicates that the CCQDs-modified ZnO NRs thin films have better crystallinity, fewer defects, and higher conductivity. Fig. 4. (c) demonstrates the photovoltaic response with and without CCQDs under 10 mW/cm2 illumination (at 380 nm). Compared to the intrinsic ZnO NRs PDs, it exhibits a higher photocurrent. This can be attributed to the conductivity of CCQDs, which results in a higher electrical conductivity of the composite thin films. This significantly reduces current losses caused by internal resistance and enables rapid collection of electron-hole pairs. It is worth mentioning that the introduction of CCQDs substantially reduces the dark current of the device, resulting in a tremendous improvement of the ratio of Iphoto/Idark by 3467.3 %, as shown in Fig. 4. (e). We analyzed the photoresponse under different light intensities (0.01 mW/cm2, 0.10 mW/cm2, 1.00 mW/cm2, 10.00 mW/cm2, 50.00 mW/cm2), as shown in Fig. 4. (d). At light intensities of 0.01 mW/cm2 and 0.10 mW/cm2, the photoelectric output of the intrinsic ZnO NRs thin films is slightly higher than that of the CCQDs-modified ZnO NRs thin films, but it does not exhibit the low dark current and high stability shown by the latter. However, under other high light intensities, the CCQDs-modified ZnO NRs thin films have advantages in terms of photocurrent, dark current, and stability. The rise and fall times (τRise, τFall) under 1 mW/cm2 light intensity are shown in Fig. 4. (f). The τRise and τFall of devices with CCQDs is 50 ms and 52 ms, while the devices without CCQDs is 55 ms and 56 ms, respectively. The response time of the PDs with CCQDs is slightly smaller than that of the PDs without CCQDs. The device's faster response time is attributed to an inherent electric field at the heterojunction, which promotes the speedy separation of photogenerated charge carriers. This facilitates the efficient drift of electrons to the N-region and holes to the P-region.

R, D*, LDR, and NEP are important parameters for evaluating PDs [53,54]. The R measure evaluates the detector's response to optical signals and is a commonly used standard for assessing the performance of PDs. Its formula is as follows [55]:
(3)
R =
I
p h
P
i l l
=
I
p h o t o
−
I
d a r k
S *
I
i l l
Here, Iphoto and Idark are light current and dark current, S is 0.08 cm2, and the incident light power of Iill is 10 mW/cm2. The R value without CCQDs is 13.5 mA/W, and after using CCQDs as a modifier, the R value is 16.72 mA/W, indicating a 24.0 % improvement. The R value indicates that ZnO NRs/CCQDs PDs have excellent performance in susceptible detection of weak light. The D* parameter measures the detector's ability to detect weak light signals under challenging environmental conditions [56]. We calculated D* using the following equation [57]:
(4)
D *
=
R *
S
i
n o i s e
=
R *
S
(
2 q *
I
d a r k
)
Where, q is the basic charge 1.6 × 10−19 C. Fig. 4. (g) displays the distribution range of D* values for devices without and with CCQDs. The average D* value for the modified device is 9.81 × 1010 Jones, whereas for the unmodified device it is 1.46 × 1010 Jones, representing a 571.9 % improvement. The maximum D* value at the lowest light intensity indicates a high capability for detecting NUV, demonstrating the suitability of CCQDs as a modifier for NUV PDs. LDR, typically expressed in decibels (dB), describes the range of light intensity that PDs can accept. The formula for LDR is [58]:
(5)
L D R = 20
log
(
I
p h o t o
*
I
d a r k
)

Among them, I*photo is the photocurrent when the light intensity is 1 mW/cm2. Fig. 4. (h) indicates no significant difference in the LDR values between devices with and without CCQDs. The average LDR of the devices modified by CCQDs is 65.32 dB, which is 90.6 % higher those without CCQDs modification. A larger LDR means that detector can handle a broader range of light intensity changes [59]. NEP represents the lowest possible incident optical power that photoelectric detector can identify as photocurrent rather than noise. NEP is related to background noise, such as thermal noise, shot noise, flicker noise, and generation-recombination noise [60]. NEP can be determined using the following equation [61]:
(6)
N E P =
i
n o i s e
R

A lower NEP indicates lower noise, indicating better device performance [62]. The noise generated by Idark is a crucial factor affecting detection capabilities. From Fig. 4. (i), it can be seen that NEP is significantly reduced after the introduction of CCQDs as a modifier. The main reason for this is the significant reduction in Idark, leading to considerable decrease in NEP. This once again demonstrates that 0-dimensional CCQDs as modifier can effectively modify the defects of 1-dimensional ZnO NRs.

The data calculated according to the above formula is shown in Table 1 ., it can be observed that the overall performance of the ZnO NRs devices modified by CCQDs can be seen to be greatly improved. In particular, D* has increased by more than fivefold, LDR has nearly doubled, and NEP has decreased significantly by 85.1 %.

In summary, this study presents a green approach to extract CQDs from waste CGs and utilize their excellent optoelectronic properties for modifying ZnO NRs thin films. This approach improves the drawbacks of ZnO-based NUV PDs, including a low D*, small LDR and high NEP. With the introduction of CCQDs, the reduction of defects in the thin films significantly improves the light scattering and light absorption capabilities, thereby greatly enhancing the performance of the device in all aspects. The optimized ZnO NRs device exhibits a high D* of 9.81 × 1010 Jones, a large LDR of 65.34 dB, and a low NEP of 2.88 × 10−12 W, demonstrating excellent overall performance. These results highlight the tremendous potential application of CCQDs in PDs, while also providing new insights for further development of low-cost ZnO-based PDs.

Yitao Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Zhiqiang Luo: Supervision, Investigation. Jinghui Li: Investigation, Formal analysis. Xuan Yu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology. Xiaoming Yu: Supervision, Methodology. Qian Qiao: Supervision, Formal analysis. Hai Zhang: Validation, Supervision. Zhenhua Li: Visualization, Supervision. Yingtang Zhou: Visualization, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: We declare that we have no financial and personal relationships with other people or organizations that can inappropriately influence our work, there is no professional or other personal interest of any nature or kind in any product, service and/or company that could be construed as influencing the position presented in, or the review of, the manuscript entitled.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was performed with the support of Zhoushan Science and Technology Bureau project (Grant No. 2023C41021, 2019C21017, 2019C21029). This work is financially supported by Foundation of State Key Laboratory of High-efficiency Utilization of Coal and Green Chemical Engineering (Grant No. 2022-K31), Zhejiang Province key research and development project (2023C01191).
